meta.name: DTS Distress Tolerance Scale
meta.description: The DTS is a 15-item self-report measure assessing an individual's perceived ability to tolerate emotional distress. Items are rated on a 5-point scale where higher scores indicate greater distress tolerance.
meta.author: Simons & Gaher (2005)
meta.reference: Simons JS, Gaher RM. The Distress Tolerance Scale: Development and validation of a self-report measure. Motivation and Emotion. 2005;29(2):83-102.
meta.instruction: Please read each statement carefully and indicate how much you agree or disagree with each. Think about how you typically feel when you are distressed or upset.

questions.text: Feeling distressed or upset is unbearable to me
When I feel distressed or upset, all I can think about is how bad I feel
My feelings of distress are so intense that they completely take over
There is nothing worse than feeling distressed or upset
I can tolerate being distressed or upset as well as most people
My feelings of distress or being upset are not acceptable
I'll do anything to avoid feeling distressed or upset
Other people seem to be able to tolerate feeling distressed or upset better than I can
Being distressed or upset is always a major ordeal for me
I am ashamed of myself when I feel distressed or upset
My feelings of distress or being upset scare me
I'll do anything to stop feeling distressed or upset
When I feel distressed or upset, I must do something about it immediately
When I feel distressed or upset, I cannot help but concentrate on how bad the distress actually feels
There is nothing I can do to make being distressed or upset feel bearable

ranges.label: Very Low Distress Tolerance
ranges.description: You have very low tolerance for emotional distress. You may feel overwhelmed when negative emotions arise and have difficulty coping. Your stress level is elevated and may be significantly affecting your physical and mental health. Seeking support and making adjustments is recommended.
suggestions: Consider working with a therapist trained in distress tolerance skills (e.g., DBT)
Learn grounding techniques for moments of acute distress
Practice mindfulness to observe emotions without judgment
Build a crisis plan with trusted supports
Seek professional counseling support

ranges.label: Low Distress Tolerance
ranges.description: You have below-average tolerance for emotional distress. Unpleasant emotions may feel difficult to manage and may lead to avoidance behaviors. At this level, stress may cause persistent tension, mood fluctuations, and reduced work efficiency or rest quality.
suggestions: Practice accepting uncomfortable emotions as temporary experiences
Try deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation when upset
Gradually expose yourself to minor discomforts to build tolerance
Consider therapy focused on emotion regulation skills
Consider discussing stress management strategies with a counselor

ranges.label: Moderate Distress Tolerance
ranges.description: You have a moderate ability to tolerate emotional distress. You can generally manage negative emotions but may struggle during particularly intense episodes.
suggestions: Continue developing your emotion regulation skills
Maintain healthy coping strategies such as exercise and social support
Practice self-compassion during difficult moments
Identify your main sources of stress and try to address them gradually
Learn time management and prioritization skills

ranges.label: High Distress Tolerance
ranges.description: You have a strong ability to tolerate emotional distress. You can generally withstand negative emotions without feeling overwhelmed or needing to escape immediately.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your resilience and coping strategies
Share your strategies with others who may benefit
Stay aware of your emotional health and seek support when needed
Continue using your effective stress management strategies
Regularly check in with yourself and engage in relaxation activities
